title: Google I/O 2026 Keynote: Gemini Intelligence Turns Android Into an Agentic OS
why_it_matters: Gemini Intelligence isn't a chatbot upgrade — it's Google embedding AI directly into the Android operating system as a proactive, cross-app execution layer. For product and UX teams, this signals a fundamental shift in how mobile interfaces will be designed: less screen-switching, more ambient automation, and new UX patterns around user confirmation flows for agentic actions. Designers will need to think about trust architecture, consent UI, and what 'agentic handoff' moments look and feel like.
whats_happening: Google I/O 2026 kicked off today, May 19, at the Shoreline Amphitheatre in Mountain View. The headline pre-announcement — Gemini Intelligence — repositions Gemini from a standalone chatbot into an intelligence layer embedded within Android itself. Google's demonstrations showed the system autonomously navigating across apps: finding a class syllabus in Gmail, identifying required textbooks, and adding them to a shopping cart, all without the user switching applications. Key features include Smart Autofill (contextual form-filling across apps), Rambler (a speech-to-text tool that removes filler words and restructures dictated speech), and Create My Widget (describe a widget in natural language; Gemini generates it on the spot, pulling data from Gmail, Calendar, and web sources). Chrome on Android is also getting agentic integration with an 'auto browse' mode that can, for example, reserve parking near an event. The full keynote — which is expected to also unveil a new Gemini model and preview Android XR smart glasses — is live-streaming today. Google is also launching Googlebooks, a new category of Android-powered laptops replacing Chromebooks, with a 'Magic Pointer' cursor that acts as an on-screen AI agent.

title: Dust Raises $40M to Make Enterprise AI 'Multiplayer' — Moving Beyond the Isolated Chatbot
why_it_matters: The 'single-player AI' problem — where each employee operates their own siloed assistant with context that vanishes after every chat — is one of the biggest structural failures in enterprise AI adoption today. Dust's multiplayer model has direct implications for product and design teams: shared agent workspaces mean UX decisions, research insights, and design rationale can compound across the organisation rather than being trapped in individual chat histories. Product leaders should watch this closely as it points toward a new class of collaborative AI tooling that teams will need to design for and within.
whats_happening: On May 18, 2026, agentic AI startup Dust announced a $40 million Series B round led by Abstract and Sequoia Capital, with participation from Snowflake Ventures and Datadog, bringing total funding to over $60 million. Dust's core thesis is that most organisations are stuck in what it calls 'single-player AI' — every employee prompts their own assistant and the context disappears into a private chat window, producing individual productivity with very little compounding effect across teams. Dust offers a shared collaboration surface where humans and AI agents work in the same persistent workspace with shared projects, context, conversations, to-dos, and a cloud-based compute environment. An intelligence layer connects over 100 enterprise data sources including Slack, Notion, Salesforce, and GitHub. The platform currently serves over 3,000 organisations with 300,000 agents deployed, 70% weekly active usage among customers, and zero reported churn in 2025. The funding will accelerate development of self-improving agents, enhanced collaboration primitives, and enterprise-scale governance infrastructure.

title: FigJam Is Now Your Coding Agent's Whiteboard — Figma Launches MCP Skills for Architecture Planning
why_it_matters: This update directly closes one of the most painful gaps in AI-assisted product development: the disconnect between what agents are building in code and what human teams can actually see, review, and react to. By letting coding agents write directly into FigJam boards, Figma is positioning itself as the shared visual layer for human-agent collaboration — not just a design tool but a live system diagram and planning canvas. For designers and cross-functional product teams, this is a major workflow unlock that brings engineering context into the collaborative space where design decisions are made.
whats_happening: Figma has shipped a suite of new MCP skills and diagramming tools for FigJam that allow AI coding agents to read and write directly to FigJam boards. The headline capability is 'figma-use-figjam', a foundational MCP skill that gives agents direct read-write access to FigJam canvases. Alongside it, a new 'generate-project-plan' workflow skill turns documents, codebases, and agent conversations into interactive visual boards automatically. The updated 'generate_diagram' tool now supports complex architecture diagrams and ERDs (Entity Relationship Diagrams) with new connector types built for database relationships; teams can also paste Mermaid.js code directly onto the canvas to render it as a diagram. A 'get_figjam' tool summarises existing FigJam boards, surfaces insights, and drafts next steps for the team. These tools currently work with MCP clients including Augment, Claude Code, Codex, Copilot in VS Code, Cursor, Factory, Kiro, and Warp. The update is currently in beta and free to use, building on the 'use_figma' MCP tool Figma shipped the previous month that lets agents create or edit designs directly on the Figma canvas using real components.

title: Salesforce Goes Headless: 'Why Should You Ever Log Into Salesforce Again?' — The UI-Optional Enterprise Is Here
why_it_matters: Salesforce Headless 360 is a landmark signal for UX and product designers: the most widely used enterprise CRM in the world has just announced that its UI is no longer the primary interface for executing work. As AI agents become the dominant users of enterprise software, the traditional screen-navigation paradigm is being replaced by API-first execution layers. Designers who work on enterprise tools, internal products, or CRM-adjacent platforms need to rethink what 'UI' means when agents, not humans, are often the ones taking action — and what the human role becomes in a world of oversight, configuration, and exception handling rather than task execution.
whats_happening: Announced in April 2026 at Salesforce's TrailblazerDX (TDX) conference and continuing to shape enterprise technology conversation this week, Salesforce Headless 360 is described by the company as the most significant architectural shift in its 25-year history. The initiative exposes every Salesforce capability — data, workflows, business logic, compliance controls — as an API, MCP tool, or CLI command. AI agents and coding tools can access and operate the full platform without opening a browser or navigating a UI. Alongside this, Salesforce launched Agentforce Vibes 2.0, a native AI development environment with multi-model support including Claude Sonnet and GPT-5, and a new Agentforce Experience Layer — a UI service that separates what an agent does from how it appears, rendering rich interactive components like flight status cards, decision tiles, and rebooking workflows natively inside Slack, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Teams, and any MCP-compatible client. Salesforce co-founder Parker Harris framed the shift bluntly: 'Why should you ever log into Salesforce again?' — positioning the traditional CRM login as a legacy behaviour on its way out.

title: CSS in 2026 Is Replacing JavaScript for UI — Customisable Selects, Native Masonry, and Scroll Animations Without a Single Line of JS
why_it_matters: For product designers and front-end engineers, this CSS evolution fundamentally changes the design-to-code conversation. Complex interactive components that once required bespoke JavaScript — animated carousels, custom dropdowns, scroll-driven transitions — are becoming achievable with pure, declarative CSS that's natively accessible and semantically correct. This reduces handoff friction, shrinks JavaScript bundle sizes, and makes AI-generated code less likely to over-engineer UI behaviour. Designers can now specify richer, more expressive interactions knowing they can be implemented leaner than ever before.
whats_happening: 2026 is shaping up to be a watershed year for CSS, with a wave of long-awaited features reaching browser stability or active development across Chrome, Firefox, and Safari. Customisable select elements (via 'appearance: base-select') now allow fully styled native dropdowns — previously impossible without rewriting the component entirely in JavaScript — while retaining built-in keyboard navigation, focus handling, and accessibility. Native CSS masonry layout (Pinterest-style grid) is progressing in Chrome Canary and Firefox, ending years of reliance on JavaScript libraries. Scroll-driven animations now allow parallax effects and scroll-triggered transitions with zero JavaScript. New 'sibling-index()' and 'sibling-count()' functions let elements know their position in the DOM and style themselves accordingly — a capability that previously required JavaScript to pass index values through components. CSS 'if()' conditional logic is also emerging, allowing state-based styling without class juggling or media query stacks. Taken together, developers characterise this as CSS completing its evolution from a 'style language' into a full 'interface engineering language', with LogRocket noting that 'the long-running divide between utility-first styling and traditional CSS is narrowing' as modern CSS features close the gap.
